Some nitpicks:

Every single parametrized test follows this exact pattern
```
  if use_precomputation:
      self._clean_preaggregation_data()
  # ... setup ...
  if use_precomputation:
      experiment.exposure_preaggregation_enabled = True
  experiment.save()
  # ...
  query_runner = ExperimentQueryRunner(
      query=experiment_query, team=self.team, force_precomputation=use_precomputation
  )
```
  That's 3 conditional blocks copy-pasted across ~11 tests. When you apply this to ratio/funnel/retention, it'll be ~40+ test methods with the same boilerplate. Consider a helper on the base class, something like:

```
  def _configure_precomputation(self, experiment, use_precomputation: bool):
      if use_precomputation:
          self._clean_preaggregation_data()
          experiment.exposure_preaggregation_enabled = True
          experiment.save()

```
<hr>

The cleanup truncates the ClickHouse table and deletes all PreaggregationJob rows. This is called at the start of each precomputed test, not in teardown. That means if a test fails partway through, stale preaggregation data could leak into the next test. Consider also calling it in teardown_method (or using addCleanup) for robustness.

Happy to approve to unblock, but I think the architecture here should evolve toward full decoupling.

The current approach bolts a heavy activity (DB checks + LLM summarization with extended thinking + Temporal workflow start) directly onto the eval workflow's hot path. Even though errors are swallowed, it still blocks the workflow for up to 120s, consumes worker activity slots, and adds an Anthropic API call that bypasses the LLM gateway.

Ideally this would be a completely separate workflow that watches for `$ai_evaluation` events — the same pattern the eval pipeline itself uses. Today the flow is:

1. Plugin server watches for `$ai_generation` events, matches conditions with compiled bytecode ([`evaluations.py:68-78`](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/master/products/llm_analytics/backend/models/evaluations.py#L68-L78)), and triggers eval workflows
2. Eval workflow runs the judge and emits a `$ai_evaluation` event to ClickHouse ([`run_evaluation.py:698-708`](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/run_evaluation.py#L698-L708) → [`emit_evaluation_event_activity`](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/run_evaluation.py#L509-L573))
3. The `$ai_evaluation` event lands in ClickHouse with all the context: verdict, reasoning, evaluation ID, trace ID, target event

A signal-emission workflow could follow the same event-driven pattern: watch for `$ai_evaluation` events where verdict is true, check the `SignalSourceConfig`, and call `emit_signal()` with the eval name + judge reasoning directly. This gives you:

- Full decoupling — eval workflow does one thing (judge + emit event), signal emission is a separate concern
- No impact on eval latency or worker slots — runs on its own queue/workers
- Consistent with existing patterns — same event-driven trigger mechanism as the eval pipeline itself
- Kill switch — can be disabled independently without touching the eval workflow
Merged for now, I agree with Andy's point on architecture. Also, we'll have to centralize the logic of configured sources into its own Kea logic

The four goals in the PR description (saturation, oversizing, load imbalance, blocking pool starvation) all seem achievable with the [`tokio-metrics`](https://crates.io/crates/tokio-metrics) crate, which is maintained by the Tokio team. It provides `busy_ratio()` built-in, min/max spread across workers for imbalance detection, and blocking pool stats — with a `metrics-rs` integration that would replace most of this file.

The main thing this custom implementation adds beyond `tokio-metrics` is **per-worker labeled metrics** (`worker="0"`, `worker="1"`, etc.). For the Rayon/Tokio decoupling goal (#47457), do we need to know *which specific worker* is hot, or is knowing the aggregate spread (min/max) sufficient? If the latter, `tokio-metrics` would cover it and significantly reduce the code here.

Per-worker labels also add Prometheus cardinality (8 workers × 7 per-worker metrics = 56 extra time series per instance), so worth considering whether we'd actually use them in dashboards.
Looking forward to seeing what we learn from these new metrics!

LGTM, minor comment, feel free to merge without an extra review.

The maxItems fix and the validator wiring look correct.

One question on the approach: `ExperimentMetric` is a discriminated union with `kind` — there's already a guard in `queries/utils.ts` that checks `metric.kind === NodeKind.ExperimentMetric`. For validating URL search params, would a lightweight structural check (discriminator + required fields) be enough here?

ajv standalone is already sharing subvalidators properly (e.g. `BreakdownFilter` is compiled once and reused across all four metric subtypes), so the ~6000 lines aren't from duplication — they're inherent to the type graph (`ExperimentFunnelMetric` alone pulls in `EventsNode`, `ActionsNode`, funnel types, etc.). Just wanted to flag the tradeoff since `validators.js` roughly doubles in size.

No objection if full schema validation is needed for a specific reason, just curious.

i think this looks good! my overall feedback though is _i wish we didn't have to do this_

if we had a better way to verify the end-user, we wouldn't need this at all

see this thread: https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C08SCAZ52S3/p1770777242381329

and this PR: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog-js/pull/3072

TL;DR ^ that feels like it might just simplify everything to the point where this is unnecessary, although it would only work for identified users (e.g. an anonymous user on a new browser would have no way to restore their previous tickets, but i don't know if that's even a valid use-case). it works like this:
- posthog generates a secret and gives it to customers
- customers generate HMAC on the distinct ID using the secret
- customer passes the hmac with calls to `identify`
- sdk passes the hmac in certain requests to posthog (e.g. here in listing tickets)
- backend computes the same hash and compares them

even if we still need this flow to support the anon-cross-browser use-case (which feels slim but i could be wrong), the above feels like a better default UX for end-users. they don't have to go re-verify themselves when they probably already just signed in to the customer's application; the customer application's auth effectively authenticates support, too.

it's all in the thread but the pushback on my PR was largely "this isn't worth the effort given the single use-case" plus a few more tours-specific concerns (like a scaling concern in my use-case where clients need to read the persons db), but there was not a lot of pushback specifically on the security approach, so it might be something we can revive if you like it
i would _love_ to have the conversation re above ^ security stuff, but nothing blocking here necessarily (and this flow might need to exist either way), so approving :)

Would like to see test cases and error handling here as this feature is fragile by the nature of reading url params which by the nature could be wild west.

Specifically the code below caught my attention:
- It's unclear what format is actually handled to let metrics be initialized given my a url param. Test cases could try out a few different serialization/deserialization formats (especially with urls and browsers there might be different encodings)
- There is also no parsing logic, which makes me believe you expect json as a param, but there is no check what the format is and also no validation on the model
- Lastly I wonder how the user experiences it if this goes wrong, but if the first two points make it robust, maybe just not prefilling the experiment is good enough as it's apparent
```
            const urlMetric = searchParams.metric
            const urlName = searchParams.name
            if (urlMetric || urlName) {
                actions.setExperiment({
                    ...NEW_EXPERIMENT,
                    metrics: urlMetric ? [urlMetric] : [],
                    name: urlName ?? '',
                })
            }
```

To enable this, we'll need a follow up to have the django API write this new Capture Sink configuration to the import config by default. Currently, it defaults to writing a kafka sink config.

Ok, good to know !
- LLMs use capture('$exception') in JS as well, it's really a pain. 
- For the double capturing, we have a mechanism in place to check if an exception has been captured already. I'll check why it's not working there. 
- For the fingerprint, we plan on improving the fingerprint in the coming weeks. It should be more reliable on exceptions without frames.

A small comment to clear up before.

There's a couple of issues that I've seen repeated in the PR so I'll just mention them as a whole here:
1. We are calling `router.values.searchParams` in many components and kea selectors, where router is the global import. I think we should be using either a `useRouter` hook in components or a selector dependency in kea to ensure reactivity works correctly no?
2. There's many places where we spread `...router.values.searchParams` directly. Should we have a whitelist to prevent random unrelated params from leaking through? Maybe not and it would cause more issues having to maintain this whitelist, up to you.
